khan_exercises.title: Three ways to end a sentence
khan_exercises.description: There are three types of terminal punctuation: a period, an exclamation point, and a question mark.
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe
Introduction to end-of-sentence punctuation and commas
Three ways to end a sentence

items.question_content:
**Which terminal punctuation mark would you use to end this sentence?** 
 
Aparna was hungry, so she ate a snack[]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **period** ends most regular sentences/statements: _“My mom ate a cookie.”_

An **exclamation point** shows emphasis like excitement, fear, or anger: _“I’m going to eat so much candy!”_ 

A **question mark** shows that you’re looking for an answer to a question: _“Is this edible?”_
This sentence is a simple statement, so it should use a **period**.

choices.content: Aparna was hungry, so she ate a snack$\redD{.}$
Aparna was hungry, so she ate a snack$\redD{!}$
Aparna was hungry, so she ate a snack$\redD{?}$

items.question_content:
**Which terminal punctuation mark would you use to end this sentence?**

Look out for that lion[]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **period** ends most regular sentences/statements: _“My mom ate a cookie.”_

An **exclamation point** shows emphasis like excitement, fear, or anger: _“I’m going to eat so much candy!”_ 

A **question mark** shows that you’re looking for an answer to a question: _“Is this edible?”_
This sentence is an urgent warning, so it should use an **exclamation mark**.

choices.content: Look out for that lion$\redD{.}$
Look out for that lion$\redD{!}$
Look out for that lion$\redD{?}$

A **question mark** shows that you’re looking for an answer to a question: _“Is this edible?”_
This sentence asks for information, so it should use a **question mark**.

choices.content: Levi, have you seen my glasses$\redD{?}$
Levi, have you seen my glasses$\redD{.}$
Levi, have you seen my glasses$\redD{!}$

khan_exercises.title: Meet the comma
khan_exercises.description: Commas separate parts of sentences from each other, and they do so in many different ways.
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe
Introduction to end-of-sentence punctuation and commas

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the comma in this sentence.**

This is Kenny my barber.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Commas** have a variety of uses, from separating items in a list to linking clauses together alongside coordinating conjunctions. One easy pattern to remember is that commas often go where someone might pause while speaking.

We'll learn about the different uses of commas in detail as the course goes on, but for now try your best!

We should separate the descriptive phrase "my barber" from the rest of the sentence:

>**This is Kenny, my barber.**

choices.content: This is Kenny$\redD{,}$ my barber.
This$\redD{,}$ is Kenny my barber.
This is$\redD{,}$ Kenny my barber.

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the comma in this sentence.**

At night we listened to the coyotes.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Commas** have a variety of uses, from separating items in a list to linking clauses together alongside coordinating conjunctions. One easy pattern to remember is that commas often go where someone might pause while speaking.

We'll learn about the different uses of commas in detail as the course goes on, but for now try your best!

We should separate the introductory phrase "At night" from the rest of the sentence:

>**At night, we listened to the coyotes.**

choices.content: At night we listened$\redD{,}$ to the coyotes.
At night we listened to$\redD{,}$ the coyotes.
At night$\redD{,}$ we listened to the coyotes.

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the comma in this sentence.**

Are you going to eat that cracker Justin?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Commas** have a variety of uses, from separating items in a list to linking clauses together alongside coordinating conjunctions. One easy pattern to remember is that commas often go where someone might pause while speaking.

We'll learn about the different uses of commas in detail as the course goes on, but for now try your best!

When addressing someone directly, we should separate their name from the rest of the sentence:

>**Are you going to eat that cracker, Justin?**

choices.content: Are you going to eat that cracker$\redD{,}$ Justin?
Are you$\redD{,}$ going to eat that cracker Justin?
Are you going to eat$\redD{,}$ that cracker Justin?

We'll learn about the different uses of commas in detail as the course goes on, but for now try your best!

We should use a comma to separate coordinate adjectives in a list:

**

If we hurry we can catch$\redD{,}$ the bus!

khan_exercises.title: Punctuating lists
khan_exercises.description: Use commas to punctuate lists of things and actions!
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe
Introduction to end-of-sentence punctuation and commas

items.question_content:
**Punctuate the different elements of this list by putting commas in the right places.**

The fruit basket was brimming with mangoes pears and apricots.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When making a list, we should place a comma between items in the list:

>_At the grocery store I bought milk, eggs, and sugar._

At Khan Academy, we like to use the Oxford (or serial) comma, which means that we put a comma before the last item of a list.
